content: The differential equation corresponding to the family of curves $y=e^x(a x+b)$ is
explanation:
The given family of curves is described by the equation $ y = e^x(ax + b) $. Let's find the corresponding differential equation:

Start with the given equation:

$ y = e^x(ax + b) $

Rewrite it using a substitution:

$ y \cdot e^{-x} = ax + b $

Differentiate both sides with respect to $ x $:

$ e^{-x} \frac{dy}{dx} - ye^{-x} = a $

Differentiate once more:

$ e^{-x} \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} - e^{-x} \frac{dy}{dx} + ye^{-x} - e^{-x} \frac{dy}{dx} = 0 $

Simplify the expression:

$ e^{-x} \left( \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} - 2\frac{dy}{dx} + y \right) = 0 $

Since $ e^{-x} \neq 0 $, we can drop it:

$ \frac{d^2y}{dx^2} - 2\frac{dy}{dx} + y = 0 $

This is the corresponding differential equation for the given family of curves.

content: If $$m$$ and $$n$$ are order and degree of the question $$\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^4+8 \frac{\left(d^2 y / d x^2\right)^3}{\left(d^4 y / d x^4\right)^5}+\left(\frac{d^4 y}{d x^4}\right)=x^2+4$$, then $$m-n$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^4+8 \frac{\left(d^2 y / d x^2\right)^3}{\left(d^4 y / d x^4\right)^5}+\left(\frac{d^4 y}{d x^4}\right)=x^2+4$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^4\left(\frac{d^4 y}{d x^4}\right)^5+8\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^3+\left(\frac{d^4 y}{d x^4}\right)^6 \\ & \quad=\left(x^2+4\right) \frac{d^4 y}{d x^4} \end{aligned}$$

So, here order $$(m)=4$$ and degree $$(n)=6$$

Then, $$m-n=-2$$

content: The solution of $$d y / d x=1+x+y+x y$$ is
explanation: $$\begin{aligned} & \text { } \frac{d y}{d x}=1+x+y+x y=(1+x)+y(1+x) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=(1+x)(1+y) \Rightarrow \int \frac{1}{(1+y)} d y=\int(1+x) d x \\ & \Rightarrow \log (1+y)=x+x^2 / 2+C \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$x d y / d x=x^2+y-2, y(1)=1$$, then $$y(2)$$ is equal to
explanation:
$$\frac{d y}{d x}-\frac{y}{x}=x-\frac{2}{x}$$

So, I.F $$=e^{-\int \frac{1}{x} d x}=e^{-\ln (x)}=\frac{1}{x}$$

Now, general solution is

$$\begin{aligned} & \quad \frac{y}{x}=\int\left(x-\frac{2}{x}\right) \frac{1}{x} d x \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}=x+\frac{2}{x}+C \\ & \because \quad y(1)=1 \\ & \therefore \quad 1=1+2+C \Rightarrow C=-2 \\ & \text { So, } \frac{y}{x}=x+\frac{2}{x}-2 \\ & \text { Hence, } y(2)=2\left(2+\frac{2}{2}-2\right)=2 \end{aligned}$$

content: The solution of differential equation $$y y^{\prime}=x\left(\frac{y^2}{x^2}+\frac{f\left(y^2 / x^2\right)}{f^{\prime}\left(y^2 / x^2\right)}\right)$$ is
explanation:
$$\frac{y}{x} \cdot \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y^2}{x^2}+\left\{\frac{f\left(\frac{y^2}{x^2}\right)}{f^{\prime}\left(\frac{y^2}{x^2}\right)}\right\}$$

Put, $$y=v x$$

So, $$v\left[v+x \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}\right]=v^2+\frac{f\left(v^2\right)}{f^{\prime}\left(v^2\right)}$$

$$\Rightarrow v x \cdot \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{f\left(v^2\right)}{f^{\prime}\left(v^2\right)} \Rightarrow 2 v \cdot \frac{f^{\prime}\left(v^2\right)}{f\left(v^2\right)} d v=\frac{2}{x} d x$$

Integrating both sides,

$$\begin{aligned} \log \left(f\left(v^2\right)\right) & =\log x^2+\log C \\ \Rightarrow \quad \log \left(f\left(v^2\right)\right) & =\log \left(C x^2\right) \Rightarrow f\left(v^2\right)=C x^2 \\ \Rightarrow \quad f\left(\frac{y^2}{x^2}\right) & =C x^2 \end{aligned}$$

content: Find the solution of equation $$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\cos (x+y)}$$
explanation:
Given, $$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\cos (x+y)}=\sec (x+y)$$

Put $$t=x+y$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \frac{d t}{d x}=1+\frac{d y}{d x} \\ & \frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x}-1 \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{d t}{d x}-1=\sec t \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{d t}{d x}=1+\sec t \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{d t}{1+\sec t}=d x \end{array}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \frac{\cos t}{\cos t+1} d t=d x \\ \Rightarrow & \frac{\cos t+1-1}{\cos t+1} d t=d x \\ \Rightarrow & \left(1-\frac{1}{1+\cos t}\right) d t=d x \end{array}$$

Taking integration both sides

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \int\left(1-\frac{1}{1+\cos t}\right) d t=\int d x+C \\ \Rightarrow & \int 1 \cdot d t-\int \frac{1}{2 \cos ^2 \frac{t}{2}} d t=x+C \\ \Rightarrow & t-\frac{1}{2} \int \sec ^2 \frac{t}{2} d t=x+C \\ \Rightarrow & x+y-\tan \frac{t}{2}=x+C \\ \Rightarrow & y=\tan \left(\frac{x+y}{2}\right)+C \end{array}$$

Given, $$\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{1}{\cos (x-y)}=\sec (x-y)$$

Put $$t=x-y$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} & \frac{d t}{d x}=1-\frac{d y}{d x} \Rightarrow \frac{d y}{d x}=1-\frac{d t}{d x} \\ \Rightarrow & 1-\frac{d t}{d x}=\sec t \Rightarrow \frac{d t}{d x}=1-\sec t \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{d t}{1-\sec t}=d x \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{\cos t}{\cos t-1} d t=d x \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{\cos t-1+1}{\cos t-1} d t=d x \end{array}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & \left(1+\frac{1}{\cos t-1}\right) d t=d x \\ \Rightarrow & \left(1-\frac{1}{1-\cos t}\right) d t=d x \\ \Rightarrow & \left(1-\frac{1}{2 \sin ^2 t / 2}\right) d t=d x \\ \Rightarrow & \left(1-\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{cosec}^2 \frac{t}{2}\right) d t=d x \end{array}$$

Taking integration on both sides,

$$\int\left(1-\frac{1}{2} \operatorname{cosec}^2 \frac{t}{2}\right) d t=\int d x+C$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \Rightarrow & t-\frac{1}{2} \frac{\left(-\cot \frac{t}{2}\right)}{1 / 2}=x+C \\ \Rightarrow & t+\cot \frac{t}{2}=x+C \\ \Rightarrow & x-y+\cot \frac{x-y}{2}=x+C \\ \Rightarrow \quad & y=\cot \left(\frac{x-y}{2}\right)+C \end{array}$$

content: The solution of the equation $$\frac{d y}{d x}+x(x+y)=x^3(x+y)^3-1$$ is
explanation:
$$\frac{d y}{d x}+x(x+y)=x^3(x+y)^3-1$$

$$\Rightarrow\left(\frac{d y}{d x}+1\right)+x(x+y)=x^3(x+y)^3$$

or $$\frac{d y+d x}{d x}+x(x+y)=x^3(x+y)^3$$

$$\begin{gathered} \text{Put}\quad x+y=v \\ \qquad d x+d y=d v \end{gathered}$$

Then, $$\frac{d v}{d x}+v x=v^3 x^3$$

or $$\quad v^{-3} \frac{d v}{d x}+v^{-2} x=x^3\quad \text{.... (i)}$$

Now, put $$v^{-2}=t$$

$$\therefore \quad-2 v^{-3} \frac{d v}{d x}=\frac{d t}{d x}$$

Then, from Eq. (i)

$$\begin{aligned} \frac{d t}{d x}-2 t x & =-2 x^3 \\ I F & =e^{\int-2 x d x}=e^{-x^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Solution is

$$\begin{aligned} & t\left(e^{-x^2}\right)=\int\left(-2 x^3\right)\left(e^{-x^2}\right) d x \\ & \Rightarrow \quad t\left(e^{-x^2}\right)=x^2 e^{-x^2}+e^{-x^2}+C \\ & \text { or } \quad t=x^2+1+c e^{x^2} \\ & \text { or } \quad \frac{1}{(x+y)^2}=x^2+1+c e^{x^2} \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: If $$p$$ and $$q$$ are order and degree of the question $$\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^4+4 \frac{\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^2}{\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)^3}+\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}=x^2-1$$, then
explanation:
Order The order of a differential equation is the highest derivative appearing in it.

Degree The degree of a differential equation is the power of the highest derivatives

Here, $$\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)^3\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^4+4\left(\frac{d^2 y}{d x^2}\right)^2+\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)^4$$

$$=\left(x^2-1\right)\left(\frac{d^3 y}{d x^3}\right)^3$$

So, here, $$p=3, r=4$$

content: The solution of $$\frac{d y}{d x}=1+x+y+x y$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} \text { Given. } \frac{d y}{d x} & =1+x+y+x y \\ & =(1+x)+y(1+x) \\ \Rightarrow \quad \frac{d y}{d x} & =(1+x)(1+y) \end{aligned}$$

On variable separable and integrating

$$\begin{aligned} & \int \frac{1}{1+y} d y=\int(1+x) d x \\ & \Rightarrow \log (1+y)=x+\frac{x^2}{2}+C \quad\left[\because \int \frac{1}{x} d x=\log (x)\right] \\ \end{aligned}$$

content: The general solution of the linear differential equation $$\frac{d y}{d x}+\sec x \cdot y=\tan x\left(0 \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}\right)$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } \frac{d y}{d x}+\sec x \cdot y=\tan x\left(0 \leq x \leq \frac{\pi}{2}\right) \\ & \begin{aligned} I F=e^{\int \sec x d x} & =e^{\log \mid \sec x+\tan x+} \\ & =|\sec x+\tan x| \end{aligned} \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ General solution is

$$\begin{aligned} & y \cdot(\mathrm{IF})=\int Q \cdot(\mathrm{IF}) d x+C \\ \Rightarrow & y \cdot|\sec x+\tan x| \\ & =\int \tan x \cdot(\sec x+\tan x) d x+C \\ \Rightarrow & y(\sec x+\tan x) \\ & =\int \tan x \cdot \sec x d x+\int \tan ^2 x d x+C \\ \Rightarrow & y(\sec x+\tan x)=\sec x+\int\left(\sec ^2 x-1\right) d x \\ \Rightarrow & y(\sec x+\tan x)=(\sec x+\tan x)-x+C \\ \Rightarrow & y=1+\frac{(C-x)}{(\sec x+\tan x)}+C^{-1}+\frac{C}{(\sec x+\tan x)}+1 \end{aligned}$$

content: On solving the differential equation $$x^2 y d x-\left(x^3+y^3\right) d y=0$$, the value of $$\log y$$ is
explanation:
$$\begin{aligned} & \text { Given, } x^2 y d x-\left(x^3+y^3\right) d y=0 \\ \Rightarrow \quad & \frac{d x}{d y}=\frac{x^3+y^3}{x^2 y} \quad \text{.... (i)}\\ \end{aligned}$$

Which is a homogeneous differential equation

Now, put $$x=v y\quad \text{.... (ii)}$$

On differentiation w.r.t to $$y$$, we get

$$\frac{d x}{d y}=v \cdot 1+y \frac{d v}{d y}$$

Then, from Eq. (i)

$$\begin{aligned} \Rightarrow \quad v+y \frac{d v}{d y} & =\frac{(v y)^3+y^3}{(v y)^2 y}=\frac{v^3+1}{v^2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad v+y \frac{d v}{d y} & =v+\frac{1}{v^2} \\ \Rightarrow \quad y \frac{d v}{d y} & =\frac{1}{v^2} \end{aligned}$$

$$\Rightarrow \quad \int v^2 d v=\int \frac{d y}{y} \quad \text { (on integrating) }$$

$$\begin{aligned} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{v^3}{3}=\log |y|-C \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \log y=\frac{x^3}{3 y^3}+C \quad \text { \{From Eq. (ii)\} } \end{aligned}$$

content: The particular solution of the differential equation $$\frac{d y}{d x}+y \cot x=2 x+x^2 \cot x$$, such that $$y(\pi / 2)=0$$ is
explanation:
Given,

$$\begin{aligned} & \frac{d y}{d x}+y \cot x=2 x+x^2 \cot x \\ & I F=e^{\int \cot x d x}=e^{\log \sin x}=\sin x \end{aligned}$$

$$\therefore$$ Complete solution is

$$\begin{aligned} & \therefore \text { Complete solution is } \\ & \quad y \cdot \sin x=\int \sin x\left(2 x+x^2 \cot x\right) d x+C \\ & \begin{aligned} \Rightarrow y \cdot \sin x=2 \int x \sin x d x+\int x^2 \cos x d x+C \\ \Rightarrow y \cdot \sin x=2 \int x \sin x d x+x^2 \sin x \\ \qquad-2 \int x \sin x d x+C \end{aligned} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad y \sin x=x^2 \sin x+C \quad \text{.... (i)} \end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{array}{ll} \because & y\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)=0 \\ \text { i.e., at } & x=\frac{\pi}{2}, \quad y=0 \end{array}$$

Then from Eq. (i),

$$\begin{aligned} \therefore \quad 0 \cdot \sin \frac{\pi}{2} & =\left(\frac{\pi}{2}\right)^2 \sin \frac{\pi}{2}+C \\ \Rightarrow \quad C & =-\frac{\pi^2}{4} \end{aligned}$$

Putting the value of $$C$$ in Eq. (i), we get

$$\begin{gathered} y \cdot \sin x=x^2 \sin x-\frac{\pi^2}{4} . \\ \Rightarrow \quad y=x^2-\frac{\pi^2}{4 \sin x} ; \quad(\sin x \neq 0) \end{gathered}$$
